title: Boundary regularity and stability for spaces with Ricci curvature bounded below
speaker: Elia Bruè
abstract: An extension of Gromov compactness theorem ensures that any family of manifolds with convex boundaries, uniform bound on the dimension and uniform lower bound on the Ricci curvature is precompact in the Gromov-Hausdorff topology. In this talk, we will describe how boundaries degenerate in the limit by studying the topological and rectifiable structure of limit spaces. This is based on a joint work with Aaron Naber and Daniele Semola.

proof_stages.label: Noncollapsed convex-boundary compactness setup
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes smooth N-dimensional manifolds with Ricci curvature bounded below, convex/nonnegative second fundamental form boundary, and a uniform noncollapsing condition, then recalls Gromov-Hausdorff compactness and Cheeger-Colding volume convergence for their limits.

proof_stages.label: RCD framework as the synthetic ambient category
proof_stages.summary: RCD(K,N) metric-measure spaces are introduced as the stable synthetic Ricci-lower-bound setting, compatible with smooth manifolds with convex boundary and therefore a natural framework for studying boundary limits.

proof_stages.label: Tangent cones and symmetry stratification
proof_stages.summary: Tangents are defined by pointed blow-ups, shown to be metric cones, and organized by k-symmetry into regular and singular strata, with special rigidity in codimensions zero and one.

proof_stages.label: Metric boundary as closure of the half-space stratum
proof_stages.summary: The boundary of a limit space is defined as the closure of S^{n-1}\S^{n-2}, equivalently the closure of points admitting a Euclidean half-space tangent, with smooth and polyhedral checks motivating the definition.

proof_stages.label: Stability of boundaries and boundary measures
proof_stages.summary: Under the hypotheses, nonempty approximating boundaries force a nonempty limit boundary, the boundaries converge in the Kuratowski sense, and their (n−1)-dimensional measures converge weakly to Hausdorff measure on the limit boundary.

proof_stages.label: Half-space epsilon regularity
proof_stages.summary: A quantitative local theorem says that if a ball in a smooth approximating space is sufficiently Gromov-Hausdorff close to a Euclidean half-space ball, then genuine boundary is present and has nearly Euclidean boundary density together with bi-Hölder coordinates.

proof_stages.label: Global structure of the limit boundary
proof_stages.summary: Using the half-space and Euclidean regularity alternatives plus the stratification, the boundary is shown to be (n−1)-rectifiable with volume/content bounds and the ambient limit is a bi-Hölder manifold with boundary away from a closed set of Hausdorff dimension at most n−2.

examples.name: Y-shaped triple junction
examples.note: An audience-proposed triple junction is used to test the tangent-cone/stratification picture and to explain informally why one-dimensional RCD cross-sections and entropy convexity exclude branching mass-merger behavior.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker and audience analyze the triple junction by looking at its symmetries/cross-section and explaining why a one-dimensional RCD cross-section would exclude it

passes.reason: A branching Y-junction is a singular non-manifold configuration raised as a forbidden enemy case for RCD/Ricci-limit structure.

examples.name: Smooth manifold with boundary
examples.note: In the smooth case the regular set is the interior and the stratum with half-space tangent is exactly the classical boundary, making the metric definition concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies the stratification/boundary definition to a smooth manifold with boundary, identifying the regular set with the interior and the singular boundary stratum with the usual boundary

passes.reason: A smooth manifold with boundary is the standard model used to check the boundary definition, not to refute a claim.

examples.name: Two-dimensional polyhedron with edges and vertices
examples.note: The polyhedron shows why the boundary definition must take the closure: edge points have half-plane tangents but vertices lie in a lower stratum and are recovered only after closing up.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker works through the polyhedron’s tangents at interior points, edge points, and vertices to justify taking the closure in the boundary definition

passes.reason: The polyhedron has singular edges and vertices and is used to show that the unclosed boundary stratum would miss vertices.
The full-dimensional polyhedral example shows that omitting the closure would miss vertices of the boundary.
The polyhedron with edges and vertices shows that omitting the closure in the boundary definition would fail to recover the topological boundary.
The polyhedron shows that omitting the closure in the boundary definition would miss the vertices.
Used to show that omitting the closure in the boundary definition would miss the vertices of a polyhedral boundary.
The polyhedron shows that omitting the closure in the boundary definition would miss vertices, so it obstructs that simpler definition.
Shows that omitting the closure would miss vertices of a polyhedral boundary, while the polyhedron is a standard stratified example.

Shows that omitting the closure would miss vertices, and has corner strata singular relative to smooth surfaces.

Shows that omitting the closure in the boundary definition would miss vertices, while the polyhedron is an ordinary surface-with-corners example.

examples.name: Shrinking cylinder converging to a segment
examples.note: This collapsing cylinder illustrates why the noncollapsing hypothesis is needed: boundary can appear in the limit away from the approximating boundaries, breaking boundary convergence.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker uses the shrinking capped cylinder concretely to trace how a sequence with no boundary can converge to a segment whose limit has boundary

passes.reason: The shrinking cylinder is a collapsed lower-dimensional limit used to refute boundary convergence without the noncollapsing assumption.

examples.name: Metric cones over nonspherical two-dimensional cross-sections
examples.note: Cones over spaces such as the projective plane illustrate possible nonmanifold singularities and the gap between abstract RCD examples and genuine Ricci-limit examples in low dimensions.
examples.engagement_reason: metric cones over nonspherical two-dimensional cross-sections are cited as nonmanifold/counterexample phenomena, but no concrete computation or construction is carried out on them

passes.reason: A cone over a nonspherical surface has a conical non-manifold tip and is invoked as a counterexample to full manifold regularity in the abstract setting.
A cone over a nonspherical surface has a singular scale-invariant apex and is used as an RCD counterexample to manifold regularity.

These cones collapse the cross-section to a nonmanifold apex and serve as obstructions to blanket manifold-regularity claims.
